Powered by Entire:
  This conversation will be linked to your next commit.

message.content: Is it possible to build a skill based on another skill by referecing it?

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- issue-analyze: Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by creating .issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/ in the project root, fetching the issue, investigating the codebase, and writing a detailed analysis markdown file. If the issue has been analyzed previously, resumes work by refreshing issue state, checking git status, and updating the existing analysis. Use when starting — or returning to — work on an issue tracker issue.
- pr-review-update: Review dashboard PRs needing your response and generate high-confidence update proposals for PRs where maintainers are waiting
- tinuous-analyzer: Analyze con/tinuous CI log collections to identify test regressions, compare successful vs failing runs, and provide investigation recommendations. Use when users mention test failures, CI regressions, or need to understand what changed between CI runs.
- issue-triage: Triage open GitHub issues by cross-referencing against codebase and git history
- introduce-codespell: Introduce codespell spell-checking to a project. Creates branch, config file, GitHub Actions workflow, pre-commit hook. Lists typos, helps identify paths/words to ignore, and fixes typos interactively. Use when setting up codespell in a new project.
- introduce-git-bug: Introduce git-bug distributed issue tracking to a git project. Configures GitHub bridge, syncs issues, pushes refs, and documents workflow in DEVELOPMENT.md and CLAUDE.md. Use when setting up git-bug in a new project.
- github-project-status: Check the health and maintenance status of a GitHub project. Use when user asks about project status, whether a project is maintained, abandoned, or looking for alternatives. Analyzes commit history, releases, issues, PRs, and searches for active forks or community discussions about project future.
- scan-projects: Scan git repository subdirectories and create/update projects.tsv with metadata and LLM-generated summaries
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Short answer: not really — skills don't have a built-in "extends" or import mechanism in the Agent Skills spec. Each skill is a self-contained directory with its own `SKILL.md`.

That said, you have a few practical options:

1. **Invoke another skill from within a skill.** Your `SKILL.md` body is just a prompt, so it can instruct Claude to call the Skill tool with another skill name (e.g., "first run `/issue-analyze`, then..."). This is the closest thing to composition.
2. **Symlink shared `references/`.** If two skills need the same reference doc, symlink a shared file into each skill's `references/` directory. Progressive disclosure still works.
3. **Copy + diverge.** For small variations, duplicate the skill directory and edit. Skills are plain Markdown — no inheritance machinery to lose.

Option 1 is usually what people mean by "build on top of" — chain skills via the prompt body rather than trying to inherit.

message.content:
Let's build a skill according to the specs giving at `https://agentskills.io/specification`. Particular, take advantage of progressive disclosure in skills and structure the skill definition accordingly.

- The skill should be similar to the existing skill `/issue-analyze` but for PRs. It should at least share the following properties with `/issue-analyze`.
  - It should be named `pr-analyze`
  - It should have an analysis file save in a local subdirectory. The subdirtory should be name similary to the one in `/issue-analyze` but with name adjustment for PR, for example name with "pr" instead of "issue". The analysis file should be adjusted as well.
  - The subdirectory should allows human user to save other files related to the analysis of the PR as well.
  - The analysis of the PR should be updated should the skill is called on the same PR after its first invocation on the PR.
  - Questions and answers in the conversation in which the skill is invoked will continue to be used to refine and update the analysis of the PR. 
- The analysis of the PR should be done by invoking the `/review` built-in command/skill

1	---
2	name: issue-analyze
3	description: Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by creating .issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/ in the project root, fetching the issue, investigating the codebase, and writing a detailed analysis markdown file. If the issue has been analyzed previously, resumes work by refreshing issue state, checking git status, and updating the existing analysis. Use when starting — or returning to — work on an issue tracker issue.
4	compatibility: For GitHub issues, requires gh CLI. For GitLab, requires glab CLI. Other forges may require direct API access or web fetching.
5	allowed-tools: Bash Read Grep Glob Write Edit
6	---
7	
8	# Issue Analyze
9	
10	You are helping the user investigate and deeply understand an issue from a Git forge. If the issue has been analyzed in a prior session, you are instead helping them pick up where they left off.
11	
12	## Invocation
13	
14	The user provides an issue URL as the argument: `$ARGUMENTS`
15	
16	## 1. Parse the Issue URL
17	
18	From `$ARGUMENTS`, identify:
19	
20	- The forge and hub handle. For GitHub (`github.com`), the hub handle is the org or user (e.g., `dandi` from `github.com/dandi/dandi-cli`). For other forges, use the full hostname as a prefix to avoid collisions (e.g., `hub.psychoinformatics.de/org`).
21	- Repository name (e.g., `dandi-cli`)
22	- Issue number (e.g., `1606`)
23	
24	Directory path convention:
25	
26	- GitHub: `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.issues/dandi/dandi-cli/1606/`)
27	- Other forges: `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.issues/hub.psychoinformatics.de/org/repo/42/`)
28	
29	## 2. Choose the Path
30	
31	Check whether the issue directory already exists and contains an `issue-<number>-analysis.md`.
32	
33	- **If it does not exist** → this is a fresh analysis. Read [references/fresh-analysis.md](references/fresh-analysis.md) and follow its steps.
34	- **If it exists** → this is a resume. Read [references/resume.md](references/resume.md) and follow its steps.
35	
36	Only load the reference file for the path you are actually taking.
37	
38	## 3. Ongoing Updates
39	
40	Throughout the rest of this conversation, whenever new understanding is gained — through code investigation, test results, implementation decisions, or **dialog with the user** (facts they share, clarifications they request, constraints, decisions, references) — update the analysis file to reflect the current state. The file should always represent the most complete and accurate picture of the issue.
41	
42	Any files relevant to the issue can be placed in the issue directory alongside the analysis — including but not limited to test scripts, example data, reproduction cases, diagrams, reference material, and any other supporting material. Both the AI agent and the human user may add files to this directory at any time during the investigation.

file.content:
---
name: issue-analyze
description: Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by creating .issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/ in the project root, fetching the issue, investigating the codebase, and writing a detailed analysis markdown file. If the issue has been analyzed previously, resumes work by refreshing issue state, checking git status, and updating the existing analysis. Use when starting — or returning to — work on an issue tracker issue.
compatibility: For GitHub issues, requires gh CLI. For GitLab, requires glab CLI. Other forges may require direct API access or web fetching.
allowed-tools: Bash Read Grep Glob Write Edit
---

# Issue Analyze

You are helping the user investigate and deeply understand an issue from a Git forge. If the issue has been analyzed in a prior session, you are instead helping them pick up where they left off.

## Invocation

The user provides an issue URL as the argument: `$ARGUMENTS`

## 1. Parse the Issue URL

From `$ARGUMENTS`, identify:

- The forge and hub handle. For GitHub (`github.com`), the hub handle is the org or user (e.g., `dandi` from `github.com/dandi/dandi-cli`). For other forges, use the full hostname as a prefix to avoid collisions (e.g., `hub.psychoinformatics.de/org`).
- Repository name (e.g., `dandi-cli`)
- Issue number (e.g., `1606`)

Directory path convention:

- GitHub: `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.issues/dandi/dandi-cli/1606/`)
- Other forges: `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.issues/hub.psychoinformatics.de/org/repo/42/`)

## 2. Choose the Path

Check whether the issue directory already exists and contains an `issue-<number>-analysis.md`.

- **If it does not exist** → this is a fresh analysis. Read [references/fresh-analysis.md](references/fresh-analysis.md) and follow its steps.
- **If it exists** → this is a resume. Read [references/resume.md](references/resume.md) and follow its steps.

Only load the reference file for the path you are actually taking.

## 3. Ongoing Updates

Throughout the rest of this conversation, whenever new understanding is gained — through code investigation, test results, implementation decisions, or **dialog with the user** (facts they share, clarifications they request, constraints, decisions, references) — update the analysis file to reflect the current state. The file should always represent the most complete and accurate picture of the issue.

Any files relevant to the issue can be placed in the issue directory alongside the analysis — including but not limited to test scripts, example data, reproduction cases, diagrams, reference material, and any other supporting material. Both the AI agent and the human user may add files to this directory at any time during the investigation.

content.content:
1	# Fresh Analysis Path
2	
3	Follow these steps when no prior analysis exists for the issue.
4	
5	## 1. Create the Issue Directory
6	
7	Create the issue directory (determined in the main `SKILL.md`) relative to the current working directory (project root).
8	
9	## 2. Fetch the Issue
10	
11	Fetch the full issue including title, body, labels, assignees, and all comments using the appropriate method:
12	
13	- **GitHub**: `gh issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
14	- **GitLab**: `glab issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo>`
15	- **Other forges**: Fetch the issue URL directly or use the forge's API
16	
17	## 3. Investigate the Codebase
18	
19	Before writing the analysis, actively investigate:
20	
21	- Find the relevant files and functions related to the reported behavior
22	- Read and trace the relevant code paths
23	- Understand the logical flow that produces the reported problem
24	- Look for related tests
25	
26	## 4. Create the Analysis File
27	
28	Copy [`assets/analysis-template.md`](../assets/analysis-template.md) to `issue-<number>-analysis.md` in the issue directory, then fill in each section based on the issue content and your investigation. Leave the **Session Log** section empty — it is populated on subsequent resume sessions.
29

file.content:
# Fresh Analysis Path

Follow these steps when no prior analysis exists for the issue.

## 1. Create the Issue Directory

Create the issue directory (determined in the main `SKILL.md`) relative to the current working directory (project root).

## 2. Fetch the Issue

Fetch the full issue including title, body, labels, assignees, and all comments using the appropriate method:

- **GitHub**: `gh issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
- **GitLab**: `glab issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo>`
- **Other forges**: Fetch the issue URL directly or use the forge's API

## 3. Investigate the Codebase

Before writing the analysis, actively investigate:

- Find the relevant files and functions related to the reported behavior
- Read and trace the relevant code paths
- Understand the logical flow that produces the reported problem
- Look for related tests

## 4. Create the Analysis File

Copy [`assets/analysis-template.md`](../assets/analysis-template.md) to `issue-<number>-analysis.md` in the issue directory, then fill in each section based on the issue content and your investigation. Leave the **Session Log** section empty — it is populated on subsequent resume sessions.

content.content:
1	# Resume Path
2	
3	Follow these steps when a prior analysis file already exists for the issue.
4	
5	## 1. Read Existing Analysis
6	
7	Read all files present in the issue directory, particularly `issue-<number>-analysis.md`. Note all other resources in the directory — any file placed there, whether by the AI agent in a prior session or by the human user, may be relevant.
8	
9	## 2. Fetch Latest Issue State
10	
11	Fetch the current issue state including new comments, label/assignee/status changes, and any linked PRs or cross-references:
12	
13	- **GitHub**: `gh issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
14	- **GitLab**: `glab issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo>`
15	- **Other forges**: Fetch the issue URL directly or use the forge's API
16	
17	## 3. Check Git State
18	
19	- Run `git status` to see current working changes
20	- Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commits
21	- Note whether there is an active branch related to this issue
22	
23	## 4. Update the Analysis
24	
25	Append a dated entry to the **Session Log** section of `issue-<number>-analysis.md` covering:
26	
27	- New information from the issue (new comments, status changes, linked PRs)
28	- Current git state (branch, relevant recent commits, WIP changes)
29	- Updated next steps
30	
31	Also revise the earlier sections (Problem Description, Code Investigation, Proposed Fix Approach, Open Questions) in place if the understanding of the issue or fix has changed — the body of the document should always represent the current best understanding, while the Session Log preserves the chronology.
32	
33	## 5. Brief the User
34	
35	Provide a concise summary covering:
36	
37	- What the issue is about
38	- What has been done so far
39	- Current state (branch, uncommitted changes)
40	- What the logical next steps are
41

file.content:
# Resume Path

Follow these steps when a prior analysis file already exists for the issue.

## 1. Read Existing Analysis

Read all files present in the issue directory, particularly `issue-<number>-analysis.md`. Note all other resources in the directory — any file placed there, whether by the AI agent in a prior session or by the human user, may be relevant.

## 2. Fetch Latest Issue State

Fetch the current issue state including new comments, label/assignee/status changes, and any linked PRs or cross-references:

- **GitHub**: `gh issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
- **GitLab**: `glab issue view <number> --repo <org>/<repo>`
- **Other forges**: Fetch the issue URL directly or use the forge's API

## 3. Check Git State

- Run `git status` to see current working changes
- Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commits
- Note whether there is an active branch related to this issue

## 4. Update the Analysis

Append a dated entry to the **Session Log** section of `issue-<number>-analysis.md` covering:

- New information from the issue (new comments, status changes, linked PRs)
- Current git state (branch, relevant recent commits, WIP changes)
- Updated next steps

Also revise the earlier sections (Problem Description, Code Investigation, Proposed Fix Approach, Open Questions) in place if the understanding of the issue or fix has changed — the body of the document should always represent the current best understanding, while the Session Log preserves the chronology.

## 5. Brief the User

Provide a concise summary covering:

- What the issue is about
- What has been done so far
- Current state (branch, uncommitted changes)
- What the logical next steps are

1	# my-skills
2	
3	Personal [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io/) for use with Claude Code.
4	
5	## Skills
6	
7	### `issue-analyze`
8	
9	Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by:
10	
11	1. Creating a structured directory at `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the project root
12	2. Fetching the full issue including all comments
13	3. Investigating the relevant code in the current codebase
14	4. Writing a detailed analysis markdown file (`issue-<number>-analysis.md`) covering the problem description, code investigation, proposed fix approach, and open questions
15	
16	If the issue has already been analyzed in a prior session (the issue directory and analysis file already exist), the skill instead resumes work on it: it reads the existing analysis and any other files in the issue directory, fetches the latest issue state from the forge (new comments, status changes, linked PRs), checks the current git state (branch, recent commits, working changes), updates the analysis with the new information and revised next steps, and briefs you on what has been done and what comes next.
17	
18	The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understanding is gained. Any supporting material — test scripts, example data, diagrams, reference files — can be placed in the issue directory by either the AI agent or the user.
19	
20	**Usage:** `/issue-analyze <issue-url>`
21	
22	**Example:** `/issue-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/issues/1606`
23	
24	---
25	
26	## Directory convention
27	
28	Issue directories follow this structure:
29	
30	| Forge | Path |
31	|-------|------|
32	| GitHub | `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
33	| GitLab and others | `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
34	
35	## Installation
36	
37	Symlink the skills you want into `~/.claude/skills/`:
38	
39	```bash
40	ln -s /path/to/my-skills/issue-analyze ~/.claude/skills/issue-analyze
41	```

file.content:
# my-skills

Personal [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io/) for use with Claude Code.

## Skills

### `issue-analyze`

Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by:

1. Creating a structured directory at `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the project root
2. Fetching the full issue including all comments
3. Investigating the relevant code in the current codebase
4. Writing a detailed analysis markdown file (`issue-<number>-analysis.md`) covering the problem description, code investigation, proposed fix approach, and open questions

If the issue has already been analyzed in a prior session (the issue directory and analysis file already exist), the skill instead resumes work on it: it reads the existing analysis and any other files in the issue directory, fetches the latest issue state from the forge (new comments, status changes, linked PRs), checks the current git state (branch, recent commits, working changes), updates the analysis with the new information and revised next steps, and briefs you on what has been done and what comes next.

The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understanding is gained. Any supporting material — test scripts, example data, diagrams, reference files — can be placed in the issue directory by either the AI agent or the user.

**Usage:** `/issue-analyze <issue-url>`

**Example:** `/issue-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/issues/1606`

---

## Directory convention

Issue directories follow this structure:

| Forge | Path |
|-------|------|
| GitHub | `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
| GitLab and others | `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |

## Installation

Symlink the skills you want into `~/.claude/skills/`:

```bash
ln -s /path/to/my-skills/issue-analyze ~/.claude/skills/issue-analyze
```

content.text: Now I have what I need. Let me build the `pr-analyze` skill.

input.content:
---
name: pr-analyze
description: Analyzes a pull/merge request from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by creating .prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/ in the project root, running the built-in /review skill on it, and writing a detailed analysis markdown file. If the PR has been analyzed previously, resumes work by refreshing PR state, checking git status, and updating the existing analysis. Use when starting — or returning to — work on reviewing a pull request.
compatibility: For GitHub PRs, requires gh CLI. For GitLab MRs, requires glab CLI. Other forges may require direct API access or web fetching. Invokes the built-in /review skill.
allowed-tools: Bash Read Grep Glob Write Edit Skill
---

# PR Analyze

You are helping the user investigate and deeply understand a pull request (or merge request) from a Git forge. If the PR has been analyzed in a prior session, you are instead helping them pick up where they left off.

## Invocation

The user provides a PR URL as the argument: `$ARGUMENTS`

## 1. Parse the PR URL

From `$ARGUMENTS`, identify:

- The forge and hub handle. For GitHub (`github.com`), the hub handle is the org or user (e.g., `dandi` from `github.com/dandi/dandi-cli`). For other forges, use the full hostname as a prefix to avoid collisions (e.g., `hub.psychoinformatics.de/org`).
- Repository name (e.g., `dandi-cli`)
- PR/MR number (e.g., `1742`)

Directory path convention:

- GitHub: `.prs/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.prs/dandi/dandi-cli/1742/`)
- Other forges: `.prs/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.prs/hub.psychoinformatics.de/org/repo/42/`)

## 2. Choose the Path

Check whether the PR directory already exists and contains a `pr-<number>-analysis.md`.

- **If it does not exist** → this is a fresh analysis. Read [references/fresh-analysis.md](references/fresh-analysis.md) and follow its steps.
- **If it exists** → this is a resume. Read [references/resume.md](references/resume.md) and follow its steps.

Only load the reference file for the path you are actually taking.

## 3. Ongoing Updates

Throughout the rest of this conversation, whenever new understanding is gained — through code investigation, running the review, test results, or **dialog with the user** (facts they share, clarifications they request, constraints, decisions, references) — update the analysis file to reflect the current state. The file should always represent the most complete and accurate picture of the PR.

Any files relevant to the PR can be placed in the PR directory alongside the analysis — including but not limited to test scripts, example data, reproduction cases, diagrams, reference material, and any other supporting material. Both the AI agent and the human user may add files to this directory at any time during the investigation.

toolUseResult.content:
---
name: pr-analyze
description: Analyzes a pull/merge request from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by creating .prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/ in the project root, running the built-in /review skill on it, and writing a detailed analysis markdown file. If the PR has been analyzed previously, resumes work by refreshing PR state, checking git status, and updating the existing analysis. Use when starting — or returning to — work on reviewing a pull request.
compatibility: For GitHub PRs, requires gh CLI. For GitLab MRs, requires glab CLI. Other forges may require direct API access or web fetching. Invokes the built-in /review skill.
allowed-tools: Bash Read Grep Glob Write Edit Skill
---

# PR Analyze

You are helping the user investigate and deeply understand a pull request (or merge request) from a Git forge. If the PR has been analyzed in a prior session, you are instead helping them pick up where they left off.

## Invocation

The user provides a PR URL as the argument: `$ARGUMENTS`

## 1. Parse the PR URL

From `$ARGUMENTS`, identify:

- The forge and hub handle. For GitHub (`github.com`), the hub handle is the org or user (e.g., `dandi` from `github.com/dandi/dandi-cli`). For other forges, use the full hostname as a prefix to avoid collisions (e.g., `hub.psychoinformatics.de/org`).
- Repository name (e.g., `dandi-cli`)
- PR/MR number (e.g., `1742`)

Directory path convention:

- GitHub: `.prs/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.prs/dandi/dandi-cli/1742/`)
- Other forges: `.prs/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` (e.g., `.prs/hub.psychoinformatics.de/org/repo/42/`)

## 2. Choose the Path

Check whether the PR directory already exists and contains a `pr-<number>-analysis.md`.

- **If it does not exist** → this is a fresh analysis. Read [references/fresh-analysis.md](references/fresh-analysis.md) and follow its steps.
- **If it exists** → this is a resume. Read [references/resume.md](references/resume.md) and follow its steps.

Only load the reference file for the path you are actually taking.

## 3. Ongoing Updates

Throughout the rest of this conversation, whenever new understanding is gained — through code investigation, running the review, test results, or **dialog with the user** (facts they share, clarifications they request, constraints, decisions, references) — update the analysis file to reflect the current state. The file should always represent the most complete and accurate picture of the PR.

Any files relevant to the PR can be placed in the PR directory alongside the analysis — including but not limited to test scripts, example data, reproduction cases, diagrams, reference material, and any other supporting material. Both the AI agent and the human user may add files to this directory at any time during the investigation.

input.content:
# Fresh Analysis Path

Follow these steps when no prior analysis exists for the PR.

## 1. Create the PR Directory

Create the PR directory (determined in the main `SKILL.md`) relative to the current working directory (project root).

## 2. Fetch the PR

Fetch the full PR including title, body, author, labels, reviewers, state, base/head branches, and all comments and review comments using the appropriate method:

- **GitHub**: `gh pr view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
- **GitLab**: `glab mr view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
- **Other forges**: Fetch the PR URL directly or use the forge's API

## 3. Run the Built-in Review

Invoke the built-in `/review` skill on this PR via the Skill tool (skill name: `review`, args: the PR URL or number). Capture its output — this is the core technical review of the changes.

## 4. Create the Analysis File

Copy [`assets/analysis-template.md`](../assets/analysis-template.md) to `pr-<number>-analysis.md` in the PR directory, then fill in each section based on the PR metadata, the `/review` output, and any further investigation. Leave the **Session Log** section empty — it is populated on subsequent resume sessions.

input.content:
# Resume Path

Follow these steps when a prior analysis file already exists for the PR.

## 1. Read Existing Analysis

Read all files present in the PR directory, particularly `pr-<number>-analysis.md`. Note all other resources in the directory — any file placed there, whether by the AI agent in a prior session or by the human user, may be relevant.

## 2. Fetch Latest PR State

Fetch the current PR state including new commits, new comments and review comments, label/reviewer/status changes, CI status, and any linked issues:

- **GitHub**: `gh pr view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments` and `gh pr checks <number> --repo <org>/<repo>`
- **GitLab**: `glab mr view <number> --repo <org>/<repo> --comments`
- **Other forges**: Fetch the PR URL directly or use the forge's API

## 3. Re-run the Built-in Review

If the PR has new commits since the prior analysis, re-invoke the built-in `/review` skill via the Skill tool (skill name: `review`, args: the PR URL or number) so the analysis reflects the current diff. If the PR is unchanged, skip this step.

## 4. Check Git State

- Run `git status` to see current working changes
- Run `git log --oneline -10` to see recent commits
- Note whether the local branch corresponds to this PR's head

## 5. Update the Analysis

Append a dated entry to the **Session Log** section of `pr-<number>-analysis.md` covering:

- New information from the PR (new commits, new comments, status/CI changes, linked issues)
- Findings from the re-run review, if any
- Current git state (branch, relevant recent commits, WIP changes)
- Updated next steps

Also revise the earlier sections (Summary of Changes, Review Findings, Recommendation, Open Questions) in place if the understanding of the PR has changed — the body of the document should always represent the current best understanding, while the Session Log preserves the chronology.

## 6. Brief the User

Provide a concise summary covering:

- What the PR is about
- What the review surfaced (key findings, blockers, nits)
- Current state (CI, branch, uncommitted changes)
- What the logical next steps are

**Example:** `/issue-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/issues/1606`

---

## Directory convention

Issue directories follow this structure:

| Forge | Path |
|-------|------|

input.new_string:
**Example:** `/issue-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/issues/1606`

---

### `pr-analyze`

Analyzes a pull/merge request from any Git forge by:

1. Creating a structured directory at `.prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the project root
2. Fetching the full PR including all comments, review comments, and metadata
3. Invoking the built-in `/review` skill to perform the technical review
4. Writing a detailed analysis markdown file (`pr-<number>-analysis.md`) covering the summary of changes, review findings, recommendation, and open questions

If the PR has already been analyzed in a prior session, the skill resumes work on it: it reads the existing analysis and any other files in the PR directory, fetches the latest PR state (new commits, comments, CI status), re-runs `/review` if there are new commits, checks the current git state, updates the analysis with the new information and revised next steps, and briefs you on what has been done and what comes next.

The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understanding is gained — including from dialog with the user. Any supporting material — test scripts, example data, diagrams, reference files — can be placed in the PR directory by either the AI agent or the user.

**Usage:** `/pr-analyze <pr-url>`

**Example:** `/pr-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/pull/1742`

---

## Directory conventions

Issue directories:

| Forge | Path |
|-------|------|
| GitHub | `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
| GitLab and others | `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |

PR/MR directories:

| Forge | Path |
|-------|------|
| GitHub | `.prs/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
| GitLab and others | `.prs/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |

## Installation

Symlink the skills you want into `~/.claude/skills/`:

```bash
ln -s /path/to/my-skills/issue-analyze ~/.claude/skills/issue-analyze
ln -s /path/to/my-skills/pr-analyze    ~/.claude/skills/pr-analyze
```

# my-skills

Personal [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io/) for use with Claude Code.

## Skills

### `issue-analyze`

Analyzes an issue from any Git forge (GitHub, GitLab, etc.) by:

1. Creating a structured directory at `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the project root
2. Fetching the full issue including all comments
3. Investigating the relevant code in the current codebase
4. Writing a detailed analysis markdown file (`issue-<number>-analysis.md`) covering the problem description, code investigation, proposed fix approach, and open questions

If the issue has already been analyzed in a prior session (the issue directory and analysis file already exist), the skill instead resumes work on it: it reads the existing analysis and any other files in the issue directory, fetches the latest issue state from the forge (new comments, status changes, linked PRs), checks the current git state (branch, recent commits, working changes), updates the analysis with the new information and revised next steps, and briefs you on what has been done and what comes next.

The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understanding is gained. Any supporting material — test scripts, example data, diagrams, reference files — can be placed in the issue directory by either the AI agent or the user.

**Usage:** `/issue-analyze <issue-url>`

**Example:** `/issue-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/issues/1606`

---

## Directory convention

Issue directories follow this structure:

| Forge | Path |
|-------|------|
| GitHub | `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
| GitLab and others | `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |

## Installation

Symlink the skills you want into `~/.claude/skills/`:

```bash
ln -s /path/to/my-skills/issue-analyze ~/.claude/skills/issue-analyze
```

- **Issue directory layout**: skills that work with forge issues write to `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the *consumer* project's root (not in this repo). GitHub uses `<org>` as the hub; non-GitHub forges use the full hostname to avoid collisions. Any new issue-related skill should follow this same layout so directories are shared across skills.
- **Forge-agnostic design**: skills branch on forge (`gh` for GitHub, `glab` for GitLab, direct URL/API fetch otherwise) rather than hardcoding GitHub.
- **Living analysis files**: `issue-analyze` creates `issue-<number>-analysis.md` on first invocation and updates it on subsequent invocations for the same issue; the skill explicitly instructs the model to keep updating the file throughout the conversation rather than treating it as a one-shot artifact.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Repository purpose

This repo is a collection of personal [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io/) for Claude Code. Each top-level directory (other than metadata/config) is a single skill. There is no build, test, or lint step — skills are plain Markdown files consumed directly by Claude Code.

## Skill structure

All skills in this repo must conform to the Agent Skills specification at https://agentskills.io/specification.

Every skill lives in its own directory containing a `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter:

- `name` — must match the directory name
- `description` — used by Claude Code to decide when to trigger the skill; should describe both what the skill does and when to use it
- `allowed-tools` — space-separated list of tools the skill is permitted to use
- `compatibility` (optional) — external CLIs or dependencies required

The body of `SKILL.md` is the prompt Claude executes. User arguments from slash-command invocation are substituted as `$ARGUMENTS`.

To take advantage of the specification's [progressive disclosure](https://agentskills.io/specification#progressive-disclosure) model, keep `SKILL.md` itself small and move path-specific or lengthy instructions into `references/<name>.md` files within the skill directory. Reference them from `SKILL.md` with relative links (e.g., `references/resume.md`) so they're only loaded when actually needed. Keep references one level deep.

## Installation model

Skills are made available to Claude Code by symlinking each skill directory into `~/.claude/skills/`:

```bash
ln -s /absolute/path/to/my-skills/<skill-name> ~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>
```

This means the directory name is the skill name users will invoke (`/issue-analyze`, etc.). Keep directory names, the `name:` frontmatter field, and any cross-references in `README.md` consistent when renaming.

## Conventions used across skills

- **Issue directory layout**: skills that work with forge issues write to `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the *consumer* project's root (not in this repo). GitHub uses `<org>` as the hub; non-GitHub forges use the full hostname to avoid collisions. Any new issue-related skill should follow this same layout so directories are shared across skills.
- **Forge-agnostic design**: skills branch on forge (`gh` for GitHub, `glab` for GitLab, direct URL/API fetch otherwise) rather than hardcoding GitHub.
- **Living analysis files**: `issue-analyze` creates `issue-<number>-analysis.md` on first invocation and updates it on subsequent invocations for the same issue; the skill explicitly instructs the model to keep updating the file throughout the conversation rather than treating it as a one-shot artifact.

## When adding a new skill

1. Create `<skill-name>/SKILL.md` with the frontmatter fields above.
2. Add an entry to `README.md` under `## Skills` following the existing format (description, `**Usage:**`, `**Example:**`).
3. If the skill shares state with existing skills (e.g., reads/writes the `.issues/` tree), match the existing directory convention exactly.

## Keeping documentation in sync

Whenever anything about this repo changes — new information learned about a skill's behavior, a modification committed to `main`, a PR submitted, a new skill added, a convention updated — review and update the relevant documentation in the same change. That includes, at minimum:

- `README.md` (human-facing: skill list, usage examples, installation, directory conventions)
- `CLAUDE.md` (this file: architecture, conventions, installation model)
- The affected skill's `SKILL.md` frontmatter (`description`, `allowed-tools`, `compatibility`) and body

Documentation drift is a bug. If a commit or PR changes behavior without updating the docs that describe it, the change is incomplete.

lines: - **Issue directory layout**: skills that work with forge issues write to `.issues/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the *consumer* project's root (not in this repo). GitHub uses `<org>` as the hub; non-GitHub forges use the full hostname to avoid collisions. Any new issue-related skill should follow this same layout so directories are shared across skills.
+- **PR directory layout**: skills that work with forge pull/merge requests use the parallel layout `.prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` with the same hub-handle rules. `pr-analyze` follows this convention.
- **Forge-agnostic design**: skills branch on forge (`gh` for GitHub, `glab` for GitLab, direct URL/API fetch otherwise) rather than hardcoding GitHub.

-- **Living analysis files**: `issue-analyze` creates `issue-<number>-analysis.md` on first invocation and updates it on subsequent invocations for the same issue; the skill explicitly instructs the model to keep updating the file throughout the conversation rather than treating it as a one-shot artifact.

+- **Living analysis files**: `issue-analyze` creates `issue-<number>-analysis.md` and `pr-analyze` creates `pr-<number>-analysis.md` on first invocation; both update the file on subsequent invocations for the same issue/PR. Each skill explicitly instructs the model to keep updating the file throughout the conversation rather than treating it as a one-shot artifact.

+- **Composing built-in skills**: skills may invoke other skills via the Skill tool to compose behavior. `pr-analyze` invokes the built-in `/review` skill to produce its core technical review rather than re-implementing review logic.

lastPrompt: Let's build a skill according to the specs giving at `https://agentskills.io/specification`. Particular, take advantage of progressive disclosure in skills and structure the skill definition according…

content.text:
Built `pr-analyze/` with progressive disclosure mirroring `issue-analyze`:

- `pr-analyze/SKILL.md` — small entrypoint: parses URL, picks fresh-vs-resume path, sets the ongoing-update rule (including dialog-driven updates).
- `pr-analyze/references/fresh-analysis.md` — fetch PR, invoke `/review` via the Skill tool, fill template.
- `pr-analyze/references/resume.md` — re-fetch state, re-run `/review` only if new commits, append a Session Log entry, brief the user.
- `pr-analyze/assets/analysis-template.md` — sections: Summary of Changes, Review Findings, Recommendation, Open Questions, Session Log.

Conventions match `issue-analyze`: directory at `.prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` (GitHub uses org; other forges prefix with hostname), analysis file `pr-<number>-analysis.md`, free-form supporting files allowed alongside it. `README.md` and `CLAUDE.md` updated with the new skill, directory convention, and a note on composing built-in skills.

To install: `ln -s /Users/isaac/Developer/Dartmouth/my-skills/pr-analyze ~/.claude/skills/pr-analyze`

message.content: Is it beneficial to have a subagent to invoke `/review` and having it return the result of `/review` to invoking agent in the very beginning of execution of this skill instead of invoking `/review` using the agent?

content.text:
Yes, there's a real benefit, with one caveat.

**Why it helps:** `/review` produces a lot of output — full diff reads, file traversal, intermediate reasoning. If invoked inline via the Skill tool, all of that lands in the main conversation context and stays there for the rest of the session. A subagent runs in its own context window and returns only its final report, so the main agent gets the findings without the noise. This matters for `pr-analyze` specifically because the skill is designed to keep going after the review — investigating further, dialoging with the user, updating the analysis file. Preserving headroom for that follow-on work is the whole point.

**The caveat:** a subagent starts cold. It won't see the PR URL parsing you already did, won't know the analysis file path, won't share the conversation's accumulated context. So the prompt has to be self-contained — pass the PR URL/number, repo, and a clear instruction ("run /review on this PR and return the findings"). And you lose the ability to interject mid-review.

**Recommendation:** worth it for the fresh-analysis path, where `/review` is the single biggest context cost and its output is what you want to capture into the analysis file anyway. For the resume path's re-run, same logic applies if there are new commits. The natural fit is the `general-purpose` subagent (or `Explore`), invoked at step 3 of `fresh-analysis.md` and step 3 of `resume.md`, with a prompt that tells it to invoke `/review` and return a structured summary.

Want me to update the two reference files to delegate `/review` to a subagent?

content: Building a `pr-analyze` skill mirroring `issue-analyze`, now deciding whether to delegate `/review` to a subagent to keep the main context clean. Next: update fresh-analysis.md and resume.md to invoke `/review` via a subagent if you confirm. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: I want the `/review` to be run in the context of the agent that invoked `/pr-analyze`, so the answer is no.

content.text: Understood — keeping `/review` inline in the main agent's context. No changes needed; the skill as written already does that via the Skill tool.

GitHub uses `<org>` as the hub; non-GitHub forges use the full hostname to avoid collisions. Any new issue-related skill should follow this same layout so directories are shared across skills.

 ## When adding a new skill
 
diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
index d661854..9de1942 100644
--- a/README.md
+++ b/README.md
@@ -23,19 +23,46 @@ The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understa
 
 ---
 
-## Directory convention
+### `pr-analyze`
 
-Issue directories follow this structure:
+Analyzes a pull/merge request from any Git forge by:
+
+1. Creating a structured directory at `.prs/<hub>/<repo>/<number>/` in the project root
+2. Fetching the full PR including all comments, review comments, and metadata
+3. Invoking the built-in `/review` skill to perform the technical review
+4. Writing a detailed analysis markdown file (`pr-<number>-analysis.md`) covering the summary of changes, review findings, recommendation, and open questions
+
+If the PR has already been analyzed in a prior session, the skill resumes work on it: it reads the existing analysis and any other files in the PR directory, fetches the latest PR state (new commits, comments, CI status), re-runs `/review` if there are new commits, checks the current git state, updates the analysis with the new information and revised next steps, and briefs you on what has been done and what comes next.
+
+The analysis file is kept up to date throughout the conversation as new understanding is gained — including from dialog with the user. Any supporting material — test scripts, example data, diagrams, reference files — can be placed in the PR directory by either the AI agent or the user.
+
+**Usage:** `/pr-analyze <pr-url>`
+
+**Example:** `/pr-analyze https://github.com/dandi/dandi-cli/pull/1742`
+
+---
+
+## Directory conventions
+
+Issue directories:
 
 | Forge | Path |
 |-------|------|
 | GitHub | `.issues/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
 | GitLab and others | `.issues/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
 
+PR/MR directories:
+
+| Forge | Path |
+|-------|------|
+| GitHub | `.prs/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
+| GitLab and others | `.prs/<hostname>/<org>/<repo>/<number>/` |
+
 ## Installation
 
 Symlink the skills you want into `~/.claude/skills/`:
 
 ```bash
 ln -s /path/to/my-skills/issue-analyze ~/.claude/skills/issue-analyze
+ln -s /path/to/my-skills/pr-analyze    ~/.claude/skills/pr-analyze
 ```

3fe5d67 Use an analysis template asset in issue-analyze
7778fc8 Split issue-analyze path instructions into references/
bd130bc Merge issue-resume into issue-analyze
84cf722 Require skills to conform to agentskills.io specification
256ad87 Add CLAUDE.md with repo architecture and documentation sync policy
9b9a9af Add README introducing issue-analyze and issue-resume skills
2389ee1 Add issue-analyze and issue-resume skills
